title: Jim The Psychic

jokes.text: In my country, they wear nothing. [...] No skates. [...] No ice. Gravel.
jokes.setup: Latka claims hockey players in his country are tougher than American players
jokes.punchline: Escalating absurdity: no padding → no clothes → no skates → no ice, just gravel
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure with perfect character voice - Latka's immigrant one-upmanship taken to absurd extremes

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: In my country, hockey players, they don't need padding. American hockey players, they wear all this padding, but in my country, no padding.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No padding? That's tough.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: That's nothing. In my country, they don't wear clothes.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No clothes?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: And no skates.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No skates? How do they play hockey without skates?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: And no ice. Just gravel.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That's not hockey, that's insanity!

jokes.text: No skates. / What do they do, slide around the ice? / No ice. Gravel.
jokes.setup: Hockey without skates seems impossible
jokes.punchline: Not just no skates, but playing on gravel instead of ice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that builds on the previous joke. The logic of removing every element of hockey one by one is perfect absurdist comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Not just no skates, but playing on gravel instead of ice

jokes.text: You call my-my stupid stuff is stupid stuff? Yeah. I think is not. I think your stupid stuff is stupid stuff, and you calling my stuff is not stupid stuff.
jokes.setup: Tony dismisses Latka's hockey story as stupid
jokes.punchline: Latka's mangled English creates a confusing loop of 'stupid stuff' logic
jokes.explanation: Character-specific humor but the wordplay is a bit labored and hard to follow

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: But Tony, is not stupid! Is true story! My cousin, he play hockey in Old Country, he fall through ice, but he okay because he wearing heavy coat!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Latka, that's the stupidest thing I ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: No, no, Tony! You not understand! The stupid thing is not the story — the stupid thing is the ice! The ice is stupid for breaking! My cousin, he not stupid. The coat, it not stupid. Only the ice is stupid!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Latka...

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: So you see, Tony, you say my story stupid, but really, only the ice is stupid. And if only the ice is stupid, then my story cannot be stupid, because my story is about stupid ice, not stupid story! So the stupid story is your story that my story is stupid!

jokes.text: Because you cannot knock me out. Because, if you do, nobody is going to like you, because I am weak and defenseless and-and cute.
jokes.setup: Tony threatens to knock out Latka
jokes.punchline: Latka uses his own weakness as emotional manipulation, adding 'cute' as the clincher
jokes.explanation: Great character work - Latka weaponizing his own innocence with perfect timing

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm gonna knock you out.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Oh no, Tony, please don't hit me. I have a very weak stomach.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: And I'm very cute when I cry.

jokes.text: See? I am winning this argument.
jokes.setup: Latka and Tony have a shouting match of 'shut up'
jokes.punchline: Latka declares victory in an argument that was just yelling
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - Latka's twisted logic delivered with confidence

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I win! I win! You see, I tell you to shut up, you tell me to shut up, but I tell you to shut up last, so I win the argument!

jokes.text: Latka draws a low card and loses the bet to stay quiet, then immediately starts celebrating how great it will be not to hear from Tony
jokes.setup: They bet on cards - loser stays quiet for a week
jokes.punchline: Latka loses but talks anyway, missing the irony entirely
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony with Latka's obliviousness to his own contradiction

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You know, Tony, I been thinking about this bet we made. I realize now that staying quiet for a week, it's not so easy for a man like me.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Latka, you lost the bet. You're supposed to be quiet.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, I know this. But Tony, I got so much to say! My feelings, they are very deep, you understand? A man cannot just keep everything inside for seven whole days.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That was the deal.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I know, I know! But Tony, you see, the bet it was not fair because nobody told me that being silent would be so difficult for someone with my personality and my culture. In my country, we express ourselves always!

jokes.text: Hey, Jim, how you doing? Uh, I-I'll get back to you on that, Bobby.
jokes.setup: Bobby asks Jim a simple greeting question
jokes.punchline: Jim treats 'how you doing' as a complex question requiring research
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim character moment - overthinking the simplest social interaction with deadpan delivery

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: How you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, that's a complex question. Let me think about that for a minute.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Physically, I'm doing okay. Emotionally, I'm a little confused. Financially, don't ask.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Spiritually, I'm searching. Mentally, I'm exhausted from answering this question.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So overall, I'd say I'm doing... fine.

jokes.text: I didn't tell you guys about it before because I didn't want you to think I was offbeat or something.
jokes.setup: Jim reveals he's psychic
jokes.punchline: He was worried about seeming 'offbeat' when he's already the strangest person they know
jokes.explanation: Good character-based irony - Jim's complete lack of self-awareness about his own weirdness

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm psychic.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're psychic?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I can read minds, see the future.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: That's amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, I was worried about telling you guys. I didn't want to seem offbeat.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Offbeat? Jim, you're already the strangest person we know.

jokes.text: You'll drink a glass of water very quickly. Somebody is going to mistake you for a girl. You'll do the cancan in a green shirt, wearing a catcher's mask. And then comes the crazy part.
jokes.setup: Jim describes his prophetic dream about Alex
jokes.punchline: Increasingly bizarre specific details, then calling the death prediction the 'crazy part'
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist escalation with perfect misdirection - the mundane details are treated as normal before the 'crazy part'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I had this dream last night about Alex. He was wearing a blue suit, standing in front of a building I'd never seen before. There was a woman with red hair next to him, and she kept laughing at something he said. Then a car pulled up—it was a '67 Cadillac, cherry red. The license plate had the numbers 3-4-7 on it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: And get this—there was a hot dog stand right across the street, and the vendor was wearing a Yankees cap. Backwards. Alex bought a hot dog with extra mustard, but no ketchup. The woman ordered a pretzel.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: And the crazy part? Alex died.

jokes.text: The worst part is that between now and the end, you're going to be at Mario's, and a beautiful, sexy blonde is going to fall into your arms. You'll be dating each other right up to the end.
jokes.setup: Jim describes the 'worst part' of Alex's impending doom
jokes.punchline: The worst part is actually a romantic fantasy most men would want
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke with Jim's twisted perspective on what constitutes bad news

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: The worst part is, it's actually a romantic fantasy most men would want.

jokes.text: After you, my lady.
jokes.setup: Jim's prediction about Alex being mistaken for a girl
jokes.punchline: Alex jokingly calls Jim 'my lady' as they leave together
jokes.explanation: Simple callback that works but doesn't add much beyond referencing the earlier setup

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Come on, my lady.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Very funny.

jokes.text: To you, he gave smarts. Banta's got muscle. Nardo's got knobs.
jokes.setup: Louie explains God's gifts to each person
jokes.punchline: Casual sexist comment about Elaine's physical attributes in a spiritual context
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate for Louie but relies on crude humor without much cleverness

jokes.text: God gave me the power to spot what everybody else has got.
jokes.setup: Louie claims God gave him a special gift
jokes.punchline: His gift is just being observant about obvious things
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Louie's inflated self-image while revealing his mundane 'gift'

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: God gave me a special gift.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I can tell things about people just by looking at them.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Like, I can tell you're a woman. I can tell he's a man. I can tell that's a cab.

jokes.text: When you first look at Ignatowski, you figure this guy must have caught God on a bad day.
jokes.setup: Louie talks about Jim seeming simple
jokes.punchline: Suggesting God was having an off day when he made Jim
jokes.explanation: Sharp, quotable line with great economy and a fresh take on the 'act of God' concept

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Jim seems simple, you know? I think God was having an off day when he made him.

jokes.text: Louie gives Alex a blessing by making random gestures and mumbling
jokes.setup: Louie offers to protect Alex with a blessing
jokes.punchline: Louie's 'blessing' is clearly improvised nonsense with made-up religious gestures
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy that works for Louie's character but is fairly predictable physical humor

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Here, let me give you a blessing for protection.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: May the spirits of the north, south, east, and west protect you. Abracadabra, hocus pocus, shazam!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, what kind of blessing is that?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The best kind! Now you're blessed.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, so far, the only sexy blonde in here is me.
jokes.setup: They're waiting for the sexy blonde from Jim's prediction to appear
jokes.punchline: Bobby identifies himself as the sexy blonde
jokes.explanation: Decent self-deprecating humor with good economy, though not particularly original

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm the sexy blonde.

jokes.text: Latka repeatedly tries to communicate while under his vow of silence, getting increasingly frustrated
jokes.setup: Latka lost the bet and must stay quiet for a week
jokes.punchline: Latka's physical attempts to communicate become increasingly desperate and ridiculous
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy that gets some mileage from the callback but isn't particularly inventive

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What's wrong with you, Latka? Cat got your tongue?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: *makes exaggerated zipping motion across lips, then pretends to swallow the key*

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Is he okay?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: He lost a bet. He's gotta stay quiet for a week.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: *starts doing an elaborate charade, pantomiming driving a taxi, picking up passengers, then suddenly clutches chest and falls to the floor*

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What is he doing now?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I think he's dying.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: *jumps up and starts writing frantically in the air with an invisible pen, then points desperately at his mouth, shakes his head vigorously*

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: This is killing me more than it's killing him.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: *climbs on a chair, dangles from the light fixture, swings back and forth while making silent screaming faces*

jokes.text: I saved up enough money after quitting drugs. [...] Jim, that's a dollar, 35. I quit drugs for a dollar, 35?
jokes.setup: Jim wants to treat Alex to an expensive dinner after quitting drugs
jokes.punchline: His entire savings from quitting drugs is $1.35
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that reveals both Jim's poverty and his minimal drug habit in a perfectly timed reveal

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Alex, I want to treat you to an expensive dinner. I've saved up all my money from quitting drugs.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's wonderful, Jim! How much have you saved?

jokes.text: Maybe this guy can see into the future. He definitely can't see anything in the present.
jokes.setup: Bobby considers whether Jim might actually be psychic
jokes.punchline: Contrasts Jim's alleged future sight with his obvious lack of present awareness
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor with great parallel structure and perfect character insight

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. If Jim can see the future, how come he can't see what's right in front of him?

jokes.text: I'm terribly sorry. That's okay, we were expecting you.
jokes.setup: Woman apologizes for falling into Alex
jokes.punchline: Alex casually says they were expecting her, referencing the prediction
jokes.explanation: Good dry delivery that acknowledges the absurdity of the prediction coming true

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm so sorry! I fell right into you.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's okay. We were expecting you.

jokes.text: Testing. Abby-dabby, icky-bicky, abby-dabby, icky-bicky.
jokes.setup: Latka gets to speak again after his week of silence
jokes.punchline: He tests his voice with nonsensical vocal exercises
jokes.explanation: Characteristic Latka weirdness that feels authentic to the character's foreign background

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Meeka meeka moo, meeka meeka moo.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Red leather, yellow leather, red leather, yellow leather.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Unique New York, unique New York, you need New York, you knead New York.

jokes.text: How is your career coming along? Well fine, as a matter of fact... Sorry to hear that, Bobby.
jokes.setup: Latka asks Bobby about his career
jokes.punchline: When Bobby says it's going well, Latka offers condolences
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection with Latka's backwards logic and great timing on the response

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: So Bobby, how is your career going?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: It's going really well, actually.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm so sorry to hear that.

jokes.text: Sunshine Cab. He just walked out the door. Can I take a message?
jokes.setup: Phone rings just as Alex leaves
jokes.punchline: Louie's matter-of-fact phone manner immediately after the dramatic exit
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of dramatic tension through Louie's mundane professionalism.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Taxi garage.

jokes.text: You got a 104 degree temperature and projectile vomiting? He's had worse dates.
jokes.setup: Woman has extreme illness symptoms as excuse to cancel date
jokes.punchline: Louie suggests Alex has had worse dating experiences than that
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that says more about Alex's dating history than the current situation

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You think that's bad? I went out with a woman once who told me she had to leave early because she was coming down with something. You know what it turned out to be? A husband.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's nothing, Louie. I once dated a woman who said she couldn't see me anymore because she was dying.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: She wasn't.

jokes.text: Hello? No, this is not Mary. Do I sound like a Mary? You have the wrong number. [...] Mary?
jokes.setup: Jim predicted someone would mistake Alex for a girl
jokes.punchline: Wrong number caller assumes Alex is Mary, fulfilling the prediction
jokes.explanation: Straightforward callback that works but doesn't add much complexity to the payoff

dialogue.speaker: Phone caller
dialogue.line: Is Mary there?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: There's no Mary here.

dialogue.speaker: Phone caller
dialogue.line: Mary Ricoletti?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No, you've got the wrong number.

dialogue.speaker: Phone caller
dialogue.line: Is this 555-4782?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yes, but there's no Mary here.

dialogue.speaker: Phone caller
dialogue.line: Oh, well you sound just like her!

jokes.text: It's happening!
jokes.setup: The prediction about Alex being mistaken for a girl comes true
jokes.punchline: Louie's panicked realization that the predictions are accurate
jokes.explanation: Standard reaction comedy that serves the plot but isn't particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... this is really happening. Alex actually looks like a girl!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, the prediction was right!

jokes.text: If I'm gonna die, I'm gonna die as I lived. Dancing the cancan in a green shirt and a catcher's mask.
jokes.setup: Alex decides to embrace the prediction rather than fear it
jokes.punchline: He claims this ridiculous costume represents how he's always lived
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor that turns a defiant moment into comedy by making the absurd seem dignified

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm not going to worry about this prediction. I'm going to embrace it. And you want to know why? Because this ridiculous costume represents exactly how I've always lived my life.

jokes.text: Ignatowski's a boob.
jokes.setup: Alex survives past the predicted time of death
jokes.punchline: Louie's understated dismissal of Jim after being terrified of his powers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie character moment - minimal words maximum attitude after his elaborate fear

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Get outta here.

jokes.text: Now you did it! I almost apologized!
jokes.setup: Alex starts to say superstitions are stupid
jokes.punchline: Louie panics that he almost showed human decency by apologizing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - apologizing is so against Louie's nature it's treated as a near disaster

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Superstitions are stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You can't say that! That's bad luck!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, superstitions aren't real.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I was about to apologize to you. Thank God I didn't. That was a close one.

jokes.text: Reiger, did you see it? It was hideous!
jokes.setup: Louie opens the door and sees something 'hideous'
jokes.punchline: It's revealed to be a little girl selling cookies, not death
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that deflates the supernatural tension with mundane reality

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What is that hideous thing?

dialogue.speaker: Little Girl
dialogue.line: Girl Scout cookies!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh thank God, I thought it was death.

jokes.text: Louie, it was a little girl selling sugar cookies. My favorite.
jokes.setup: Louie describes the girl as hideous
jokes.punchline: Alex reveals it's something completely harmless and his favorite
jokes.explanation: Solid deflation of tension but fairly predictable once the setup is established

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A girl? You're telling me you went out with a girl like that?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What do you mean, 'a girl like that'?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I mean she's hideous! She's got a face like a mud fence!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, that's my mother.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Your mother? I thought you said you went out with a girl!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I did. We went to dinner. She's my favorite girl in the whole world.

jokes.text: Oh my God, a ghost. [...] A talking ghost. [...] A reasonable ghost. [...] A working ghost. [...] A courteous ghost!
jokes.setup: Jim sees Alex alive and assumes he must be a ghost
jokes.punchline: Each of Alex's normal behaviors becomes increasingly impressive for a ghost
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that perfectly captures Jim's twisted logic and gets funnier with each addition

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Alex? You're... you're a ghost!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm not a ghost, Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, no, it's okay. I can see right through you... well, not literally, but—look, you're walking around, you're talking. That's amazing for a ghost!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Jim, I'm alive.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: And now you're contradicting me? Ghosts can do that? Boy, ghosts have really upgraded their skill set.

jokes.text: A reasonable ghost. / A working ghost. / A courteous ghost!
jokes.setup: Jim continues thinking Alex is a ghost
jokes.punchline: Jim cataloging the ghost's surprisingly mundane qualities
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that builds the absurdity. Jim's fascination with a polite ghost is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, so you're a ghost. That explains a lot. But I gotta say, for a supernatural entity, you're remarkably ordinary. You complain about your feet hurting, you're worried about your electric bill, you keep asking me if I've seen your keys.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I expected chains, moaning, maybe some ectoplasm. Instead, you're just... regular. You're like the most boring haunting in history.

tmdb_overview: Jim announces he has psychic dreams and unfortunately, he has had one about Alex, culminating in his death. The gang becomes increasingly worried about Alex, when one by one, all of the events Jim dreamed about start coming true. However, Alex continues to reject Jim's superstitions. Louie becomes so worried about Alex, he comes to Alex's apartment to take him somewhere (so the final part of Jim's dream doesn't come true, since he is to meet his doom that evening at home), but possibly to spite Louie, Alex decides to stay and tempt fate. In the end, it turns out that the doom Jim dreamed about was a girl scout knocking at Alex's door, instead of his death, but when Louie leaves, Alex actually appears very relieved that Jim's premonition was wrong.
insight: Jim's psychic con escalates beautifully across 33 jokes in 50 minutes of pure character comedy.
